critique.summary: The article provides an accessible introduction to Bayesian statistics for data scientists, contrasting it with frequentist statistics through examples and practical applications. It explains key concepts such as priors, likelihoods, posteriors, and credible intervals, and demonstrates Bayesian inference with Python code using the PyMC library.
mainPoints: Frequentist statistics treats parameters as fixed and data as random, while Bayesian statistics treats parameters as random variables with uncertainty.
Bayesian inference uses Bayes' theorem to update prior beliefs about parameters based on observed data, resulting in a posterior distribution that quantifies uncertainty.
The article uses a die-rolling example to illustrate differences between frequentist and Bayesian approaches, showing how Bayesian credible intervals provide probabilistic interpretations.
Bayesian methods can incorporate prior knowledge to improve inference, especially when data is sparse or noisy.
Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithms, such as NUTS used in PyMC, enable practical computation of posterior distributions for complex models.
Bayesian linear regression can handle outliers and regularization naturally through appropriate priors, linking classical regularization methods to Bayesian priors.
Bayesian statistics offers a flexible and powerful framework for modeling uncertainty in data science applications.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately explains fundamental concepts of Bayesian and frequentist statistics, including the philosophical differences and mathematical formulations. The die example and Bayesian updating are correctly described, and the use of PyMC for Bayesian inference is consistent with current best practices. The explanation of credible intervals versus confidence intervals is also accurate. The discussion on MCMC methods and their role in Bayesian computation is factually correct. Overall, the article provides a truthful and well-founded introduction to Bayesian statistics without making unsupported claims.
considerations: The article assumes some familiarity with probability and statistics, which may limit accessibility for complete beginners.
While the article discusses the impact of priors, it does not deeply explore challenges such as prior sensitivity or model misspecification.
The computational complexity and convergence diagnostics of MCMC methods are mentioned briefly but could be elaborated for practical use.
The article focuses on Bayesian methods in data science but does not compare performance or limitations relative to frequentist methods in real-world scenarios.
Ethical considerations or potential biases introduced by subjective priors are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the practical use and philosophical differences between frequentist and Bayesian statistics. Some commenters share personal experiences and frustrations with Bayesian methods, while others highlight the evolving integration of both approaches in modern statistics.

comments.author: jhbadger
comments.text: I think Rafael Irizarry put it best over a decade ago -- while historically there was a feud between self-declared "frequentists" and "Bayesians", people doing statistics in the modern era aren't interested in playing sides, but use a combination of techniques originating in both camps: https://simplystatistics.org/posts/2014-10-13-as-an-applied-...
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects the current consensus in statistics that the strict division between frequentist and Bayesian methods is less relevant, and practitioners often combine approaches pragmatically.

comments.author: statskier
comments.text:
I went through grad school in a very frequentist environment. We “learned” Bayesian methods but we never used them much.

In my professional life I’ve never personally worked on a problem that I felt wasn’t adequately approached with frequentist methods. I’m sure other people’s experiences are different depending on the problems you gravitate towards.

In fact, I tend to get pretty frustrated with Bayesian approaches because when I do turn to them it tends to be in situations that already quite complex and large. In basically every instance of that I’ve never been able to make the Bayesian approach work. Won’t converge or the sampler says it will take days and days to run. I can almost always just resort to some resampling method that might take a few hours but it runs and gives me sensible results.

I realize this is heavily biased by basically only attempting on super-complex problems, but it has sort of soured me on even trying anymore.

To be clear I have no issue with Bayesian methods. Clearly they work well and many people use them with great success. But I just haven’t encountered anything in several decades of statistical work that I found really required Bayesian approaches, so I’ve really lost any motivation I had to experiment with it more.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a valuable practical perspective on the challenges of applying Bayesian methods to complex problems, though it may underrepresent Bayesian successes in certain domains.

comments.author: lottin
comments.text:
> In Bayesian statistics, on the other hand, the parameter is not a point but a distribution.

To be more precise, in Bayesian statistics a parameter is random variable. But what does that mean? A parameter is a characteristic of a population (as opposed to a characteristic of a sample, which is called a statistic). A quantity, such as the average cars per household right now. That's a parameter. To think of a parameter as a random variable is like regarding reality as just one realisation of an infinite number of alternate realities that could have been. The problem is we only observe our reality. All the data samples that we can ever study come from this reality. As a result, it's impossible to infer anything about the probability distribution of the parameter. The whole Bayesian approach to statistical inference is nonsensical.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a common misunderstanding of Bayesian statistics; while Bayesian parameters are treated as random variables to represent uncertainty, this does not imply multiple realities but a formal probabilistic framework.

comments.author: hawtads
comments.text: I think it would be interesting if frequentist stats can come up with more generative models. Current high level generative machine learning all rely on Bayesian modeling.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly notes the prominence of Bayesian methods in generative modeling within machine learning, though frequentist approaches to generative models also exist but are less common.
